sections.title: Part 1: Multiple Choice (Reading)
sections.instructions: You are going to read three extracts which are all concerned in some way with communication. For questions 1–6, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text.
sections.passageHtml:
Extract 1: Language Learning

I was inspired to describe my approach to language learning after reading a book on the subject by Tim Barnham. The method he describes is very similar to the one that I use, though I take issue with a couple of his suggestions, and I think his method needs to be updated for the internet age. Nevertheless I recommend the book highly, as it’s full of good suggestions. Please note I’m not an expert. I can only comment on my own experiences, and how some tech know-how has helped me pursue my chief pleasure in life: learning languages.

I generally take the internet for granted as an important tool in my daily life, but I still have those ‘wow!’ moments every now and then. When I’m corresponding, talking or video-chatting with a person on the other side of the world, learning about their culture or what they eat for breakfast, the power of technology is really driven home to me. This experience goes completely hand-in-hand with using the net to learn a language; it’s what the net is all about.

Extract from a novel: Chapter One

There are eleven other children in Grania’s class. Eight of them know sign language. Grania soon learns that even though she is encouraged every day to use her voice she is barely understood. She resolves to keep her voice inside, not to let it out. But her teacher, Miss Amos, won't settle for that. She taps Grania on the shoulder, watches her lips, brings Grania’s attention back to her own lips to see the shapes of the words and to practise them. She instructs Grania in the single-hand alphabet, which Grania, already knowing her printed letters, has no trouble learning. She also shows her how to use the signing space in front of her neck and upper chest.

Outside of class, the other children signal to one another with animation. Grania sends signals out slowly from her body, but she is frustrated by the flap and flurry of hands that face her when signals try to come back in. At times she sees nothing more than a rapid blur. Instead, she focuses on lips, as she had done at home. But in class, her teacher wants more: she wants Grania to see that a word is about to form, to guess what it will mean. This works in slow motion with Miss Amos, whom Grania now tries to please, but away from the classroom the other children expect her to understand not lips but hands, and at their speed. If Grania does not understand, she is left out.

Extract: Non-verbal Communication: Where Nature Meets Culture

The search for the biological foundation of human culture inevitably leads to non-verbal communication. Intuition suggests that such communication has been an essential element of the evolution of humans as social beings. Without it, the diversity and sophistication of today’s social systems would be unimaginable. However, there is the opposite hypothesis that the evolution of non-verbal communication may in part be the result of our being thoroughly social entities: the social nature of humans may have amplified the evolution of a capacity we share with other primates but developed to a degree unequalled by any other species.

As far as language is concerned, these issues have been subject to fascinating research in linguistics, biological anthropology and other fields. However, the fundamental question of what led to humans becoming social beings and thus, ultimately, the basis of human culture, remains contested between the disciplinary territorial claims of biology and the social sciences. Quite obviously, the presumed interdependence of the evolution of non-verbal communication as a biological function and the evolution of its significance for human culture calls for an interdisciplinary effort to explore the processes involved. One promising approach is the study of similarities and differences of non-verbal communication among humans and non-humans (above all, primates).

questionGroups.instruction: Choose the correct letter A, B, C or D.

questions.instruction: 1. What is the writer’s main purpose in the first paragraph?
questions.explanation: Chọn D — 'to explain his motivation for writing the article'. Ở câu đầu tiên, tác giả viết: 'I was inspired to describe my approach to language learning after reading a book on the subject by Tim Barnham.' Cụm 'I was inspired to describe...' biểu thị động lực/lý do tác giả viết bài viết này (được truyền cảm hứng sau khi đọc sách của Barnham). ❌ A sai vì các kỹ năng công nghệ chỉ được nhắc đến như là công cụ hỗ trợ ở cuối đoạn chứ không phải mục đích viết chính; ❌ B sai vì tác giả chỉ giới thiệu cuốn sách và khuyên đọc chứ không nói phần nào của cuốn sách gây ấn tượng nhất; ❌ C sai vì lợi ích của việc học ngôn ngữ không phải trọng tâm chính được giải thích trong đoạn 1. Source: CAE 3, Test 2, Page 34 Method: read both sides of the gap and choose the option that fits meaning, collocation and grammar. Trap: reject distractors that are close in meaning but do not fit the pattern or register in context.
questions.answerLocation: D. to explain his motivation for writing the article. Evidence cue: use this answer-key option with the passage/audio paraphrase; confirm the full claim, not just a repeated keyword.
options: A. to describe which technology skills helped him learn languages
B. to say which part of Barnham’s book impressed him most
C. to indicate how he benefits from learning a language
D. to explain his motivation for writing the article
questions.source: Cambridge Certificate in Advanced English 3, Cambridge University Press, Part 1: Multiple Choice (Reading)

questions.instruction: 2. What does the writer say about the internet in the second paragraph?
questions.explanation: Chọn C — 'He is not always aware of how useful he finds it.'. Tác giả viết ở câu đầu đoạn 2: 'I generally take the internet for granted as an important tool in my daily life, but I still have those ‘wow!’ moments every now and then.' Thành ngữ 'take something for granted' có nghĩa là coi điều gì đó là hiển nhiên và mặc định có sẵn, không thực sự nhận thức hết giá trị hoặc trân trọng nó thường xuyên. Điều này khớp với việc 'không phải lúc nào cũng nhận thức được mức độ hữu ích của nó'. ❌ A sai vì tác giả sử dụng internet rất tốt; ❌ B sai vì mặc dù có những giây phút ngạc nhiên, tác giả không nói rằng mình hoàn toàn không thể hiểu nổi làm sao sống thiếu nó ngày xưa; ❌ D sai vì tác giả trân trọng và tận hưởng internet chứ không hề ghét bỏ. Source: CAE 3, Test 2, Page 34 Method: read both sides of the gap and choose the option that fits meaning, collocation and grammar. Trap: reject distractors that are close in meaning but do not fit the pattern or register in context.
questions.answerLocation: C. He is not always aware of how useful he finds it.. Evidence cue: use this answer-key option with the passage/audio paraphrase; confirm the full claim, not just a repeated keyword.
options: A. He wishes that he was able to make better use of it.
B. He cannot understand how he once managed without it.
C. He is not always aware of how useful he finds it.
D. He dislikes how important it has become in his daily life.
questions.source: Cambridge Certificate in Advanced English 3, Cambridge University Press, Part 1: Multiple Choice (Reading)

questions.instruction: 3. What does the reader learn about Grania’s teacher?
questions.explanation: Chọn D — 'She will not allow Grania to remain silent.'. Đoạn 1 viết về phản ứng của Grania khi tiếng nói của mình không được hiểu rõ: 'She resolves to keep her voice inside, not to let it out. But her teacher, Miss Amos, won't settle for that. She taps Grania on the shoulder, watches her lips, brings Grania’s attention...' Cụm 'won't settle for that' (không chấp nhận điều đó, tức là không chấp nhận việc Grania quyết giữ tiếng nói bên trong/im lặng) chứng minh cô giáo Amos không cho phép cô bé im lặng mà bắt buộc cô bé phải giao tiếp và thực hành phát âm cũng như ký hiệu. ❌ A sai vì cô giáo vẫn khuyến khích cả giọng nói lẫn ký hiệu; ❌ B và C không có thông tin chi tiết chứng minh trong bài. Source: CAE 3, Test 2, Page 35 Method: read both sides of the gap and choose the option that fits meaning, collocation and grammar. Trap: reject distractors that are close in meaning but do not fit the pattern or register in context.
questions.answerLocation: D. She will not allow Grania to remain silent.. Evidence cue: use this answer-key option with the passage/audio paraphrase; confirm the full claim, not just a repeated keyword.
options: A. She thinks that Grania must express herself better with her hands.
B. She expects Grania to make rapid progress.
C. She spends most of her time with Grania.
D. She will not allow Grania to remain silent.
questions.source: Cambridge Certificate in Advanced English 3, Cambridge University Press, Part 1: Multiple Choice (Reading)

questions.instruction: 4. What does the writer suggest about the other children in Grania’s class?
questions.explanation: Chọn A — 'They have limited patience with Grania’s difficulties.'. Ở cuối đoạn 2 có câu: '...but away from the classroom the other children expect her to understand not lips but hands, and at their speed. If Grania does not understand, she is left out.' Các bạn cùng lớp mong đợi Grania phải hiểu được ngôn ngữ ký hiệu ở tốc độ giao tiếp tự nhiên của chúng. Nếu Grania không hiểu kịp, chúng sẽ bỏ mặc cô bé (left out) chứ không kiên nhẫn làm chậm lại hay giải thích, cho thấy sự kiên nhẫn rất hạn chế của chúng đối với khó khăn của cô bé. ❌ B và C không xuất hiện trong bài; ❌ D sai vì chúng không chủ động từ chối nói chuyện ngay từ đầu, chỉ là do Grania không bắt kịp tốc độ nên mới bị để lại ngoài lề cuộc trò chuyện. Source: CAE 3, Test 2, Page 35 Method: read both sides of the gap and choose the option that fits meaning, collocation and grammar. Trap: reject distractors that are close in meaning but do not fit the pattern or register in context.
questions.answerLocation: A. They have limited patience with Grania’s difficulties.. Evidence cue: use this answer-key option with the passage/audio paraphrase; confirm the full claim, not just a repeated keyword.
options: A. They have limited patience with Grania’s difficulties.
B. They are jealous of Grania’s relationship with the teacher.
C. They mock Grania when her use of sign language is unclear.
D. They are reluctant to include Grania in any of their conversations.
questions.source: Cambridge Certificate in Advanced English 3, Cambridge University Press, Part 1: Multiple Choice (Reading)

questions.instruction: 5. What does ‘a capacity’ in line 8 refer to?
questions.explanation: Chọn B — 'non-verbal communication'. Ở câu trước, tác giả nói về 'evolution of non-verbal communication' (sự tiến hóa của giao tiếp phi ngôn ngữ). Câu tiếp theo viết: 'the social nature of humans may have amplified the evolution of a capacity we share with other primates...' Cụm từ 'a capacity' (một năng lực) được dùng để thay thế cho cụm từ 'non-verbal communication' ở phía trước. ❌ A, C, D đều không phù hợp ngữ pháp và ngữ nghĩa thay thế cho một năng lực/khả năng được chia sẻ giữa con người và linh trưởng. Source: CAE 3, Test 2, Page 35 Method: read both sides of the gap and choose the option that fits meaning, collocation and grammar. Trap: reject distractors that are close in meaning but do not fit the pattern or register in context.
questions.answerLocation: B. non-verbal communication. Evidence cue: use this answer-key option with the passage/audio paraphrase; confirm the full claim, not just a repeated keyword.
questions.source: Cambridge Certificate in Advanced English 3, Cambridge University Press, Part 1: Multiple Choice (Reading)

questions.instruction: 6. What does the writer say about the development of humans as social beings?
questions.explanation: Chọn A — 'Its causes are disputed.'. Tác giả nhận định trong đoạn 2: 'However, the fundamental question of what led to humans becoming social beings and thus, ultimately, the basis of human culture, remains contested between the disciplinary territorial claims of biology and the social sciences.' Cụm 'remains contested' (vẫn đang bị tranh cãi/chưa ngã ngũ) giữa sinh học và khoa học xã hội đồng nghĩa với 'causes are disputed' (các nguyên nhân gây ra sự phát triển này vẫn đang được tranh luận). ❌ B sai vì tầm quan trọng của nó không bị xem nhẹ; ❌ C sai vì có rất nhiều nghiên cứu thú vị ('subject to fascinating research') trong các lĩnh vực; ❌ D sai vì bài viết không nói vai trò của giao tiếp phi ngôn ngữ bị hiểu lầm. Source: CAE 3, Test 2, Page 35 Method: read both sides of the gap and choose the option that fits meaning, collocation and grammar. Trap: reject distractors that are close in meaning but do not fit the pattern or register in context.
questions.answerLocation: A. Its causes are disputed.. Evidence cue: use this answer-key option with the passage/audio paraphrase; confirm the full claim, not just a repeated keyword.
options: C. Little research has been carried out into the processes involved.
D. The role played by non-verbal communication is misunderstood.
questions.source: Cambridge Certificate in Advanced English 3, Cambridge University Press, Part 1: Multiple Choice (Reading)

sections.title: Part 2: Gapped Text (Reading)
sections.instructions: You are going to read an extract from a magazine article about music. Six paragraphs have been removed from the extract. Choose from the paragraphs A–G the one which fits each gap (7–12). There is one extra paragraph which you do not need to use.
sections.passageHtml:
WORLD MUSIC: CHEIKH LÔ

Cheikh Lô’s eclectic music is inspired by African rhythms, salsa and pop, says Nigel Williamson.

The man who has just made the best world music album of the year is sitting on a rocky outcrop overlooking the Atlantic Ocean, his hair blowing gently in the African breeze. We are only a kilometre from the centre of Dakar but the bustling Senegalese capital seems a world away. ‘I come here in the middle of the night when I need to be alone,’ says Cheikh Lô.

[ Gap 7 ]

Now established as one of the most exciting names in world music, he has just returned after a week spent promoting his latest album, which was co-produced by his friend and mentor N’Dour and the record manager Nick Gold.

[ Gap 8 ]

The resulting blend of sounds is warm and lyrical, relaxed yet passionate, a kaleidoscope of apparently simple rhythms that build into complex and mesmerising patterns. Yet, despite this talent, Lô’s passage has not been easy and he has had to wait until his forties for recognition. Born in a village in Burkina Faso, he grew up speaking the tribal languages of Wolof and Bambara and hearing traditional African music played.

[ Gap 9 ]

By the time he was a teenager the international language of pop music had infiltrated village life, not only in assimilated local styles but in American forms too. ‘I saw American and British singers performing in Africa. I learnt to play Beatles songs on the guitar. So my influences were African, salsa and pop.’

[ Gap 10 ]

And that was what he continued to do. A few years later he migrated to Paris, home to many African musicians, and spent two years playing sessions there. ‘I was a studio drummer, I played in a French band and in another reggae band. It was a confusing period. There was so much to learn and I had no money.’

[ Gap 11 ]

Yet it was to be five years before they began. First, the enigmatic Lô disappeared for three years. ‘Everybody thought I had given up music but I went to study with my spiritual guide,’ Lô explains. His faith still lies at the roots of his music.

[ Gap 12 ]

Hence it was late 1996 before the world got to hear Lô’s album Ne La Thiass. It was a timeless, mostly acoustic affair, whereas the follow-up possesses a tougher, more cosmopolitan feel. Much of the newfound drive and energy comes from the horn arrangements of Pee Wee Ellis. ‘When he came to Dakar to work with us it was like a homecoming for everyone,’ Lô says.

The last word on Lô goes to his mentor and champion. ‘He’s got energy and rhythm and he’s got his own musical story,’ N’Dour says. ‘He’s the man to take African music to the world.’

Paragraphs options (A–G):

A. The recording should earn Lô a hearing far beyond the traditional world-music audience. Still rooted in the swaying Senegalese rhythms known as mbalax, it is also laced with funky horns and a touch of Afro-Cuban fire.

B. These included the legendary Cuban flautist Richard Egües, who turned out to have been an early hero. ‘I fell in love with his music as a kid when I heard him playing with Orquesta Aragon. I had never heard the flute played like that,’ Lô says.

C. By the time Lô returned with a batch of songs, N’Dour was otherwise engaged, promoting his global hit Seven Seconds with singer Neneh Cherry.

D. But his older brothers also had access to imported Cuban salsa records. ‘We didn’t understand what they were singing about but we related to the swing and rhythm,’ he says.

E. As a result of this exposure, he was able to get a job when he moved to Senegal at the age of 25. There he joined the house band at Dakar’s best hotel, The Savana, playing drums and singing bland pop for tourists. ‘But it was an education in different sorts of music. I didn’t go to music school, so I had to learn on the job.’

F. So he returned to Senegal, where he finally got to make his first record in 1990, a low-cost cassette of the kind that dominates the African market but which never sees the light of day elsewhere. Eventually N’Dour heard him playing at a Dakar club and offered to produce an album intended to launch Lô on an international stage.

G. Lô is a devout man. He is also the possessor of one of the world’s great voices. Three years ago on his London debut, The Times reviewed the concert under the headline ‘First rays of an African star’.

questionGroups.instruction: Select the correct paragraph (A–G) for each gap.

sections.title: Part 3: Multiple Choice (Reading)
sections.instructions: You are going to read an extract from a book. For questions 13–19, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text.
sections.passageHtml:
Introduction to a book about maps

This book is a celebration of the map in its myriad forms over time. I have attempted to penetrate beneath the sometimes glossy, sometimes plain surface to look at why they came into being, who their creators were and what their relationship was to the society in which they were created. All this effort may seem excessive for an object which for many people is just an ephemeral item of passing need, though there seems always to have been an equal number for whom maps have been an endless source of fascination on scientific, aesthetic, historical and cultural grounds.

Over the past twenty years most people working on the academic study of maps have come to accept the definition formulated in 1987 by Harley and Woodward at the beginning of the first volume of their monumental and still incomplete History of Cartography that ‘Maps are graphic representations that facilitate a spatial understanding of things, concepts, conditions, processes or events in the human world’. This is the definition I have borne in mind in this book. In 1987, though, it caused some consternation among those who took a more restrictive view of the map as a primarily utilitarian geographical paper object, whose quality was to be assessed primarily in terms of its geometrical accuracy.

This traditional view of the historical development of mapping was propagated primarily by geographers, some of whom had only a passing acquaintance with historical method, with occasional contributions by librarians and map dealers who were anxious to increase popular interest in their area. Historians of discovery also used old maps to illustrate their texts, sometimes doing research into their creation and publication histories. This practice, and the traditional view of geographers, led to a lasting confusion between the history of exploration and the history of cartography which has proved over-restrictive to both, and beneficial to neither.

Geographical researchers tended to view the evolution of mapping in terms of the gradual victory of objective ‘truth’ and precise scientific method over geographical ignorance. The achievements of national mapping agencies were lauded as the pinnacles of cartographic achievement. The creators of European mapping from before 1500 were condemned, by most academic geographers, as ignorant or at least befuddled, and many of its most appealing aspects dismissed as ‘mere decoration’. Meanwhile mainstream historians assumed that maps were the province of academic geographers. In so far as they took any interest, it was in the form of modern historical mapping that clarified their interpretation of the past.

The detailed study of old maps was relegated to local historians and librarians. The typical fruits of their labours were long lists of maps and the different states in which they were to be found. Much of this work was fascinating in its own right and was essential for any further progress. But it was marked by diffidence and quite often a lack of intellectual rigour. It was as if the authors did not have the collective courage to say out loud what their emotions told them loud and clear: that scientific measurement was not the be all and end all of mapping.

It is only in recent years this has radically changed. This is partly due to the increasing awareness of the importance of the visual, as a consequence of the spread of television and the internet, and the ease with which images can be manipulated in a digital environment. But also, thanks to the growing popularity of interdisciplinary studies at university, academic historians of all types began to find that maps sometimes offered perspectives on their subjects that were not possible from other sources. It is accepted that for some purposes, such as navigation and administration, mathematical accuracy did and still does play a major role in cartography. Conversely, the very aspects that tended to be disregarded, such as the distortions and the decoration, become of enormous significance since they can give particularly precious insights into the mentalities of past ages.

For many enthusiasts, the fascination of maps ironically stems from the fact that they are pieces of fiction. With historical military maps, for example, the truth dawns that the map they are interested in does not accurately depict the shapes of the fields where battles occurred. Yet it has to be that way. Given the impossibility of representing the total reality, with all its complexity, on a flat surface, hard decisions have to be taken as to what features to select for accurate representation. For most of the time, this process of selection is almost instinctive. The mapmaker knows the purpose that he wants his map to serve, and beyond that he is unwittingly guided by the values and assumptions of the time in which he lives.

In order to meet that purpose, the information that is represented will be prioritized by the mapmaker — and not necessarily in accordance with actual geographical size. Even on modern national topographic mapping such features as motorways will be shown far larger than they actually are because they are important to drivers, and users will expect to see them without difficulty.

questions.instruction: 13. What does the writer suggest about his book in the first paragraph?
questions.explanation: Chọn B — 'Some enthusiasm for the subject may be needed to appreciate it.'. Tác giả viết ở cuối đoạn 1: 'All this effort may seem excessive for an object which for many people is just an ephemeral item of passing need, though there seems always to have been an equal number for whom maps have been an endless source of fascination...' Việc dành nhiều tâm huyết viết cuốn sách này có thể là quá đà đối với người chỉ cần dùng bản đồ qua loa tạm thời, nhưng sẽ rất đáng giá và được trân trọng bởi những người có niềm đam mê sâu sắc với bản đồ. Từ đó thấy được độc giả cần có sẵn sự hứng thú/đam mê để hiểu và đánh giá cao cuốn sách này. ❌ A sai vì tác giả chỉ nói mình cố gắng làm sáng tỏ chứ không than phiền khó thực hiện; ❌ C sai vì không đề cập phong cách trình bày không đồng đều; ❌ D sai vì người ta không chỉ dùng nó như tài liệu tra cứu thuần túy. questions.instruction: 16. What connection does the writer make in the fourth paragraph between geographers and mainstream historians?
questions.explanation: Chọn B — 'They were both sceptical about the value of using older historical maps.'. Trong khi đó, các nhà sử học chính thống lại giả định bản đồ là độc quyền của nhà địa lý và họ chỉ hứng thú với bản đồ lịch sử hiện đại vẽ lại quá khứ ('assumed that maps were the province of academic geographers... in the form of modern historical mapping'). Như vậy, cả hai nhóm này đều thiếu tin tưởng hoặc coi nhẹ giá trị nghiên cứu trực tiếp từ các bản đồ cổ. ❌ A, C, D đều không đúng thực tế mô tả ở đoạn 4. questions.instruction: 18. What point is the writer making about maps as ‘pieces of fiction’ in line 37?
questions.explanation: Chọn D — 'A totally accurate representation of an area is unachievable.'. Tác giải thích: 'Given the impossibility of representing the total reality, with all its complexity, on a flat surface, hard decisions have to be taken as to what features to select for accurate representation.' Do không thể thể hiện được toàn bộ thực tế phức tạp trên một bề mặt phẳng, việc vẽ bản đồ bắt buộc phải dựa trên sự chọn lọc chủ quan của người vẽ, khiến bản đồ không bao giờ phản ánh chính xác 100% thế giới thực và do đó được ví như những tác phẩm hư cấu ('pieces of fiction'). ❌ A sai vì tính hư cấu này không phải để tăng sự phổ biến; ❌ B, C không phản ánh đúng ý kiến của tác giả. questions.instruction: 19. What point is exemplified by the reference to motorways?
questions.explanation: Chọn C — 'the mapmaker’s recognition of the social context.'. Trong đoạn cuối, tác giả cho biết các đường cao tốc (motorways) được vẽ lớn hơn kích thước địa lý thực của chúng vì chúng rất quan trọng đối với các tài xế và người dùng bản đồ mong muốn nhìn thấy chúng dễ dàng. Việc điều chỉnh tỉ lệ vẽ dựa trên nhu cầu và thói quen thực tế của cộng đồng sử dụng bản đồ thể hiện người vẽ bản đồ nhận thức và phản ánh bối cảnh xã hội (social context). ❌ A sai vì đây là quy chuẩn vẽ bản đồ chuyên nghiệp vì lợi ích người dùng chứ không phải cảm tính cá nhân; ❌ B sai vì việc phóng to không mang tính tỉ mỉ tiểu tiết; ❌ D sai vì không liên quan đến cập nhật thời sự bản đồ. sections.title: Part 4: Multiple Matching (Reading)
sections.instructions: You are going to read an article in which four naturalists explain their choice of most inspiring book about the environment. For questions 20–34, choose from the naturalists (A–D). The naturalists may be chosen more than once.
sections.passageHtml:
Wildlife Books

We invited four leading naturalists to tell us about the wildlife classic that has influenced them most.

A. Geoffrey Lean

At least it wasn’t hard to choose the author. As an environmental journalist, one advantage of longevity is that I have had the chance to meet some of the giants who pioneered thinking in the field. Of these, none stood, indeed, still stands, taller than Barbara Ward. I can’t think of anyone else more at the heart of environmental issues. She has synthesised her experience of various environmental movements into her own compelling philosophy. Unwillingly ‘volunteered’ to cover the field, I found, as a young journalist, that she, more than anyone, made it all make sense.

Picking the book was much harder. It could have been Only One Earth or Progress for a Small Planet. But The Home of Man is, to me, Barbara’s most important book. Its focus is on the explosive growth of the world’s cities, but its canvas is the great themes to which she devoted her life. It is as eloquent and as impassioned a plea as exists for what we would now call ‘sustainable human development’. In the hundreds of books I have read since, I have yet to meet its equal.

B. Linda Bennett

When I open the pages of Signals for Survival by Niko Tinbergen, I can hear the long calls of herring gulls, recall the smell of the guano in the hot sun and visualise the general hullabaloo of the colony. This book explains superbly, through words and pictures, the fascinating world of animal communication.

Read Signals for Survival and then watch any gull colony, and the frenzy of activity changes from apparent chaos to a highly efficient social structure. You can see which birds are partners, where the boundaries are and, later on in the season, whole families can be recognised.

Niko Tinbergen’s collaboration in this book with one of this century’s most talented wildlife artists, Eric Ennion, was inspirational and has produced a book of interest to anyone with a love of wildlife. His spontaneous style of painting came from years of watching and understanding birds. With just a minimal amount of line and colour, he brings to life how one gull is an aggressor, how another shows appeasement. This is the art of a true field naturalist.

C. Lee Durrell

Most definitely, My Family and Other Animals by my husband Gerald Durrell is the book that has had the greatest influence on my life.

I was doing research work into animal vocalisations in Madagascar when I first read the book. I had been there two years and was discouraged by the number of setbacks I was encountering but when, at the end of the day, I opened My Family and Other Animals to where I had left off the night before, the world became a brighter place. Animals, people, joy and beauty inextricably woven together — a microcosm of a world worth saving.

Many people say that our species is the worst because of the terrible things we have done to the others. But I like to think back to Gerald as a boy in My Family and Other Animals, looking at the world’s inhabitants as a whole, a family whose members, be they good, bad or indifferent, are nevertheless so intertwined as to be inseparable. And that is a concept we all need to grasp.

D. Bruce Pearson

A copy of The Shell Bird Book, by James Fisher, found its way into my school library shortly after it was first published in 1966. I was drawn to it at once, especially to the 48 colour plates of birds by Eric Ennion, painted, as the jacket puts it, ‘... with particular skill and charm’. It was those Ennion images which captured my attention.

I already had copies of other bird books and had spent several holidays learning to identify birds. They encouraged me to begin sketching what I saw as an aid to identification. But in The Shell Bird Book there was so much more to feast on. As well as the glorious Ennion paintings, there were chapters on migrants and migration, a review of the history of birds in Britain, and, best of all, a chapter on birds in music, literature and art.

It was the broad span of ornithological information and the exciting images that steered me towards being more of a generalist in my appreciation of birds and the natural world. The book made it clear that my emotional and creative response to nature was as valid and as possible as a rational and scientific one. And, as art was a stronger subject for me than maths or physics, I began to see a door opening for me.

sections.instructions: For questions 1–12, read the text below and decide which answer (A, B, C or D) best fits each gap. There is an example at the beginning (0).
sections.passageHtml:
Driving from Beijing to Paris

‘Every (0) journey begins with a single step.’ We might (1) ..... this proverb for the annual 16,000 km Beijing to Paris car rally, and say that every rally begins with a turn of the wheel. From China, several hundred courageous men and women will (2) ..... out for Paris in pursuit of what, for many, is likely to prove an impossible (3) ..... . Everybody is prepared for the worst and organiser Philip Young expects a high drop-out (4) ..... , especially on the rally’s difficult first (5) ..... across central China and over the high mountain (6) ..... of the Himalayas. ‘If twenty-five cars (7) ..... it to Paris, we’ll be doing well,’ he says.

The first Beijing-Paris car rally took place in 1907. It was won by Prince Borghese, an Italian adventurer, who crossed the (8) ..... line just a few metres (9) ..... of the only other car to complete the race. At the time his achievement was (10) ..... as comparable to that of the great explorer, Marco Polo.

According to the rules, all the cars in the rally must be more than thirty years old, which means that the rough roads and high altitude are a (11) ..... test of both the cars and the drivers. A sense of adventure is essential. One driver said, ‘Our (12) ..... is to have a good time, enjoy the experience and the magnificent scenery — and the adventure of a lifetime.’

questionGroups.instruction: Choose the correct letter A, B, C or D.

questions.explanation: Chọn A — 'adapt'. Ta dùng 'adapt a proverb/saying' mang nghĩa phỏng theo, sửa đổi một câu tục ngữ cho phù hợp với tình huống/ngữ cảnh mới. ❌ B 'moderate' (tiết chế, làm dịu đi); ❌ C 'improve' (cải thiện); ❌ D 'form' (hình thành) đều không đi kèm 'proverb' để tạo ra nghĩa thích hợp trong trường hợp này. Source: CAE 3, Test 2, Page 44 Method: read both sides of the gap and choose the option that fits meaning, collocation and grammar. Trap: reject distractors that are close in meaning but do not fit the pattern or register in context.
questions.answerLocation: A. adapt. Evidence cue: use this answer-key option with the passage/audio paraphrase; confirm the full claim, not just a repeated keyword.
questions.source: Cambridge Certificate in Advanced English 3, Cambridge University Press, Part 1: Multiple-Choice Cloze

questions.explanation: Chọn C — 'set'. Cụm phrasal verb 'set out for + somewhere' nghĩa là khởi hành, bắt đầu lên đường đi đâu đó. ❌ A 'head' (thường đi trực tiếp với giới từ 'for' chứ không dùng 'head out for' theo cách chỉ hành trình chính thức này); ❌ B 'move' (di chuyển); ❌ D 'try' (thử). Source: CAE 3, Test 2, Page 44 Method: read both sides of the gap and choose the option that fits meaning, collocation and grammar. Trap: reject distractors that are close in meaning but do not fit the pattern or register in context.
questions.answerLocation: C. set. Evidence cue: use this answer-key option with the passage/audio paraphrase; confirm the full claim, not just a repeated keyword.
questions.source: Cambridge Certificate in Advanced English 3, Cambridge University Press, Part 1: Multiple-Choice Cloze

questions.explanation: Chọn D — 'dream'. Cụm từ 'impossible dream' (giấc mơ bất khả thi, giấc mơ không thể thực hiện được) là một kết hợp từ (collocation) vô cùng tự nhiên và phổ biến trong tiếng Anh. ❌ A 'vision' (tầm nhìn); ❌ B 'fantasy' (ảo tưởng kì quặc); ❌ C 'hope' (hy vọng). Source: CAE 3, Test 2, Page 44 Method: read both sides of the gap and choose the option that fits meaning, collocation and grammar. questions.explanation: Chọn A — 'rate'. Cụm danh từ ghép 'drop-out rate' có nghĩa là tỷ lệ bỏ cuộc giữa chừng (trong một cuộc đua, cuộc thi) hoặc tỷ lệ học sinh bỏ học. ❌ B 'number' (số lượng); ❌ C 'speed' (tốc độ); ❌ D 'frequency' (tần suất). questions.explanation: Chọn B — 'stage'. Từ 'stage' được dùng để chỉ một chặng đường, phân đoạn của một cuộc đua dài (như đua xe rally, đua xe đạp). Ở đây chỉ chặng đầu tiên khó khăn qua miền trung Quốc. ❌ A 'period' (giai đoạn thời gian); ❌ C 'time' (thời gian); ❌ D 'round' (vòng đấu, thường dùng trong các giải thể thao đối kháng hoặc vòng loại). questions.explanation: Chọn B — 'passes'. Cụm 'mountain passes' có nghĩa là các đèo núi, lối đi hiểm trở vượt qua núi cao. Đây là thuật ngữ chính xác trong địa lý để chỉ lối vượt qua dãy Himalayas. ❌ A 'crossings' (nơi giao lộ, ngã tư hoặc chỗ băng qua sông); ❌ C 'directions' (hướng đi); ❌ D 'passages' (hành lang, lối đi trong tòa nhà hoặc đoạn văn). questions.source: Cambridge Certificate in Advanced English 3, Cambridge University Press, Part 1: Multiple-Choice Cloze

questions.explanation: Chọn D — 'make'. Cấu trúc 'make it to + somewhere' là một thành ngữ mang nghĩa đến được nơi nào đó một cách thành công sau khi trải qua nhiều khó khăn. ❌ A 'get' (cần giới từ 'get to' chứ không dùng 'get it to' theo nghĩa này); ❌ B 'take' và ❌ C 'have' đều không tạo thành cấu trúc phù hợp. questions.explanation: Chọn D — 'finishing'. Cụm từ cố định 'finishing line' có nghĩa là vạch đích của một cuộc đua. ❌ A 'closing' (khép lại); ❌ B 'final' (cuối cùng); ❌ C 'ending' (kết thúc) đều không được sử dụng để ghép với 'line' tạo thành vạch đích cuộc đua. questions.explanation: Chọn B — 'ahead'. Ta có cấu trúc 'ahead of someone/something' nghĩa là dẫn trước, ở phía trước ai đó/cái gì đó. ❌ A 'forward' (về phía trước, không dùng với 'of'); ❌ C 'front' (cần dùng cấu trúc 'in front of'); ❌ D 'advance' (thường dùng trong cụm 'in advance of' mang ý nghĩa thời gian: trước thời hạn). Source: CAE 3, Test 2, Page 44 Method: read both sides of the gap and choose the option that fits meaning, collocation and grammar. Trap: reject distractors that are close in meaning but do not fit the pattern or register in context.

questions.explanation: Chọn C — 'regarded'. Ta dùng cấu trúc bị động 'be regarded as + something' mang nghĩa được xem như là, được coi là. ❌ A 'thought' (đòi hỏi cấu trúc 'be thought to be'); ❌ B 'referred' (cần đi với giới từ 'referred to as'); ❌ D 'noted' (không hợp ngữ cảnh mang tính đánh giá cao ngang tầm vóc lịch sử). Source: CAE 3, Test 2, Page 44 Method: read both sides of the gap and choose the option that fits meaning, collocation and grammar. questions.explanation: Chọn C — 'severe'. Cụm 'severe test' có nghĩa là một cuộc thử nghiệm/thử thách vô cùng khốc liệt, khắc nghiệt và cam go đối với cả người và phương tiện. ❌ A 'firm' (chắc chắn, vững vàng); ❌ B 'strict' (nghiêm khắc, dùng cho luật lệ, kỷ luật); ❌ D 'grave' (nghiêm trọng, thường dùng cho tình huống xấu hoặc bệnh tật). questions.explanation: Chọn A — 'aim'. Danh từ 'aim' nghĩa là mục tiêu, mục đích hướng tới: 'Our aim is to V...' (Mục tiêu của chúng tôi là...). ❌ B 'target' (mục tiêu mang tính số liệu cụ thể cần đạt hoặc bia bắn); ❌ C 'proposal' (đề xuất, kiến nghị); ❌ D 'intent' (ý định, mang sắc thái pháp lý hoặc văn phong trang trọng). sections.instructions: For questions 13–27, read the text below and think of the word which best fits each gap. Use only one word in each gap. There is an example at the beginning (0).
sections.passageHtml:
Central Park

If you have the chance (0) TO take a walk through Central Park in New York, you will get a quick tour of the wide range of cultures and people who live in the city. (13) ..... man speeds along on a racing bike singing (14) ..... the top of his voice, (15) ..... dances to the beat of techno music coming from a tape recorder.

Central Park, the first public park built in America, allows for just about (16) ..... conceivable leisure activity in a rectangle of just over one and a half square kilometres. But it may (17) ..... that its best use is for the most entertaining sport in New York — people watching. Visitors can have (18) ..... better introduction to the diversity of New York than a stroll in this park.

Central Park did not always embrace (19) ..... a variety of human life. Having won a competition for the park’s design in 1858, Frederick Law Olmsted and Calvert Vaux envisaged the place (20) ..... an oasis of calm in a disorderly city. The idea (21) ..... to create a place where the upper-class citizens of the city could take gentle exercise (22) ..... being disturbed. However, the park authorities never managed to enforce a regime of order. Olmsted (23) ..... been determined to create the illusion of the countryside in the heart of New York. The fact that skyscrapers are now visible (24) ..... the tops of the park’s tallest trees (25) ..... certainly have horrified him. But this contrast between country and city landscape is (26) ..... gives the park (27) ..... very own special charm.

sections.title: Part 3: Word Formation
sections.instructions: For questions 28–37, read the text below. Use the word given in capitals at the end of some of the lines to form a word that fits in the gap in the same line. There is an example at the beginning (0).
sections.passageHtml:
Why new technology sometimes fails

In recent decades, many inventors have come up with (0) REVOLUTIONARY new products that have promised to change the way we live. Some, like mobile phones and laptop computers, have certainly done that, but the majority have completely failed to live up to everybody’s (28) ..... and have quickly been forgotten. So what went wrong? (29) ..... , there are four tests that any new invention must pass to ensure its (30) ..... .

First of all, is there sufficient consumer demand? If nobody wants the product, then no matter how brilliant the idea behind it, it’s not going to take off. Secondly, commercial (31) ..... is always very much related to how (32) ..... the product is. The costlier it is initially, the less likely it is to become a big seller, (33) ..... of how interesting and desirable it might seem.

(34) ..... is another important element. After all, if a product is so complex that non-technical salespeople are (35) ..... of explaining how it works and what it can do for potential customers, then it will be hard to sell.

Finally, any new gadget or domestic appliance needs to be both reliable and (36) ..... . If it doesn’t do what the salesperson claims, and do so (37) ..... over a period of time for its users, then word will soon get round and nobody else will buy it.

questionGroups.instruction: Form the correct word from the prompt given in CAPITALS.

acceptedAnswers: failed to live up to everybody's expectations
đáp ứng được sự kỳ vọng của ai đó. Vị trí đi sau sở hữu cách 'everybody's' yêu cầu một danh từ.
questions.explanation: EXPECT (động từ: mong đợi, kỳ vọng) → expectations (danh từ số nhiều: những sự kỳ vọng). Ta có cụm từ cố định 'live up to someone's expectations' nghĩa là đạt được/đáp ứng được sự kỳ vọng của ai đó. Vị trí đi sau sở hữu cách 'everybody's' yêu cầu một danh từ. Source: CAE 3, Test 2, Page 47 Method: complete the gap with "EXPECTATIONS" only after checking the words before and after it. Trap: keep the sentence grammatical and avoid answers that fit the topic but break the structure.
questions.answerLocation: failed to live up to everybody’s expectations
questions.source: Cambridge Certificate in Advanced English 3, Cambridge University Press, Part 3: Word Formation

Trap: keep the sentence grammatical and avoid answers that fit the topic but break the structure.

sections.title: Part 4: Gapped Sentences
sections.instructions: For questions 38–42, think of one word only which can be used appropriately in all three sentences. Write the word in CAPITAL LETTERS.
sections.passageHtml:
⚠️ Dạng bài riêng CAE (không có trong CPE)

Mỗi câu hỏi gồm 3 câu riêng biệt. Bạn phải tìm MỘT từ duy nhất điền được vào cả 3 câu. Từ đó có thể mang nghĩa khác nhau hoặc là phần của cụm từ khác nhau trong mỗi câu.

questionGroups.instruction: Write one word which fits all three sentences.

questions.instruction:
Q38.
• Mrs Lorenzo ______ me a copy of the book she had written many years ago.
• The results of the survey ______ that people are eating more healthily than five years ago.
• Fiona ______ no interest in science when she was at school.
questions.explanation: Đáp án: SHOWED (động từ chỉ hành động ở quá khứ của SHOW). • Câu 1: 'Mrs Lorenzo showed me a copy...' = Bà Lorenzo đã cho tôi xem/đưa tôi một bản sao cuốn sách bà viết. • Câu 2: 'The results of the survey showed that...' = Kết quả khảo sát cho thấy rằng... (động từ chỉ thông tin hiển thị/chứng minh). • Câu 3: 'Fiona showed no interest...' = Fiona không thể hiện chút hứng thú nào đối với khoa học. Ta có cụm từ 'show interest in' (thể hiện sự quan tâm đối với). 💡 Lưu ý: Phải chia động từ ở dạng quá khứ đơn 'showed' để phù hợp với ngữ cảnh thời gian quá khứ trong các câu. Source: CAE 3, Test 2, Page 48 Method: complete the gap with "SHOWED" only after checking the words before and after it. Trap: keep the sentence grammatical and avoid answers that fit the topic but break the structure.
questions.answerLocation: Source gap: Write one word which fits all three sentences. Q38. • Mrs Lorenzo ______ me a copy of the book she had written many y.... Correct completion: SHOWED.
questions.source: Cambridge Certificate in Advanced English 3, Cambridge University Press, Part 4: Gapped Sentences

questions.instruction:
Q39.
• You might as well do your homework now; you'll have to do it at some ______ .
• The problem with our Geography teacher is that it takes him half an hour to get to the ______ of the lesson.
• Don’t get upset by what Carlo says; politeness isn’t really his strong ______ .
questions.explanation: Đáp án: POINT (danh từ). • Câu 1: 'at some point' = vào một thời điểm nào đó, một lúc nào đó trong tương lai. • Câu 2: 'get to the point of the lesson' = đi thẳng vào trọng tâm/ý chính của bài học. • Câu 3: 'strong point' = điểm mạnh, sở trường (lịch sự không phải là sở trường của anh ấy). 💡 Mẹo: POINT là một danh từ cực kỳ đa nghĩa, xuất hiện trong nhiều cụm từ giao tiếp thông dụng hàng ngày. Source: CAE 3, Test 2, Page 48 Method: complete the gap with "POINT" only after checking the words before and after it. Trap: keep the sentence grammatical and avoid answers that fit the topic but break the structure.
questions.answerLocation: Source gap: Write one word which fits all three sentences. Q39. • You might as well do your homework now; you'll have to do it at.... Correct completion: POINT.
questions.source: Cambridge Certificate in Advanced English 3, Cambridge University Press, Part 4: Gapped Sentences

questions.instruction:
Q40.
• The government needs to ______ major financial and political decisions in the coming year.
• It ______ much intelligence to realise that the plan to ban parking in the city centre won’t work.
• I don’t think we really have much choice at this stage — all we can ______ is our accountant’s advice.
acceptedAnswers: Đòi hỏi nhiều trí thông minh để nhận ra... Cấu trúc 'It takes + noun
follow advice' nghe theo lời khuyên.
questions.explanation: Đáp án: TAKE (động từ). • Câu 1: 'take major decisions' = đưa ra các quyết định lớn. Cụm từ cố định: take/make a decision. • Câu 2: 'It takes much intelligence...' = Cần/Đòi hỏi nhiều trí thông minh để nhận ra... Cấu trúc 'It takes + noun/time + to-V'. • Câu 3: 'take our accountant's advice' = làm theo lời khuyên của kế toán. Ta có cụm 'take/follow advice' (nghe theo lời khuyên). Source: CAE 3, Test 2, Page 48 Method: complete the gap with "TAKE" only after checking the words before and after it. Trap: keep the sentence grammatical and avoid answers that fit the topic but break the structure.
questions.answerLocation: Source gap: Write one word which fits all three sentences. Q40. • The government needs to ______ major financial and political de.... Correct completion: TAKE.
questions.source: Cambridge Certificate in Advanced English 3, Cambridge University Press, Part 4: Gapped Sentences

questions.instruction:
Q41.
• The tutor said the organisation of ideas in my essay was ______ , but I needed to do more work on the conclusion.
• They say that the secret of a ______ skin is drinking plenty of liquids and eating lots of fruit and vegetables.
• It was a long time before the road was ______ of traffic, so that she could overtake the lorry.
questions.explanation: Đáp án: CLEAR (tính từ). • Câu 1: 'essay was clear' = bố cục ý tưởng trong bài luận rất rõ ràng, mạch lạc, dễ hiểu. • Câu 2: 'clear skin' = làn da láng mịn, khỏe mạnh, sạch mụn. • Câu 3: 'clear of traffic' = con đường đã thông thoáng, không còn bị tắc nghẽn xe cộ nữa. Source: CAE 3, Test 2, Page 49 Method: complete the gap with "CLEAR" only after checking the words before and after it. Trap: keep the sentence grammatical and avoid answers that fit the topic but break the structure. Language rule: key word transformation keeps the key word unchanged while preserving meaning through grammar pattern and lexical control.
questions.answerLocation: Source gap: Write one word which fits all three sentences. Q41. • The tutor said the organisation of ideas in my essay was ______.... Correct completion: CLEAR.
questions.source: Cambridge Certificate in Advanced English 3, Cambridge University Press, Part 4: Gapped Sentences

questions.instruction:
Q42.
• The band got its first big ______ in 2003 when it played in America.
• It’s rained hard for two days and our trip won’t go ahead unless there’s a ______ in the weather.
• Sam and Anna went to Spain for a short ______ in September.
questions.explanation: Đáp án: BREAK (danh từ). • Câu 1: 'first big break' = cơ hội lớn đầu tiên giúp thay đổi cuộc đời/sự nghiệp nghệ thuật. • Câu 2: 'a break in the weather' = thời tiết chuyển biến, có một khoảng tạnh ráo sau chuỗi ngày mưa liên tục. • Câu 3: 'a short break' = kỳ nghỉ ngắn ngày. Source: CAE 3, Test 2, Page 49 Method: complete the gap with "BREAK" only after checking the words before and after it. Trap: keep the sentence grammatical and avoid answers that fit the topic but break the structure. Language rule: key word transformation keeps the key word unchanged while preserving meaning through grammar pattern and lexical control.
questions.answerLocation: Source gap: Write one word which fits all three sentences. Q42. • The band got its first big ______ in 2003 when it played in Ame.... Correct completion: BREAK.
questions.source: Cambridge Certificate in Advanced English 3, Cambridge University Press, Part 4: Gapped Sentences

sections.title: Part 5: Key Word Transformation
sections.instructions: For questions 43–50, complete the second sentence so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence, using the word given. Do not change the word given. You must use between three and six words, including the word given.
sections.passageHtml:
Example:

0 Fernanda refused to wear her sister’s old dress.

Q43. The reduction in the price of the magazines led to a growth in sales.
RESULT
Sales of the magazine ______ the reduction in the price.
acceptedAnswers: WENT UP AS A RESULT OF
GREW AS A RESULT OF hoặc INCREASED
questions.explanation: Đáp án: GREW AS A RESULT OF (hoặc INCREASED / WENT UP AS A RESULT OF). 📐 Cấu trúc: Chuyển đổi giữa cấu trúc nguyên nhân - kết quả: 'A led to B' (A dẫn đến B) ↔ 'B occurred as a result of A' (B xảy ra như là kết quả của A). • Câu gốc: 'led to a growth in sales' (quá khứ đơn) → chuyển thành động từ quá khứ 'grew' (tăng trưởng), 'increased' (tăng), hoặc 'went up' (đi lên). • Từ khóa 'RESULT' bắt buộc phải dùng trong cụm giới từ 'as a result of' (bởi vì, là kết quả của). questions.instruction:
Q44. I think the weather will improve next week.
CHANGE

questions.explanation: Đáp án: A CHANGE FOR THE. 📐 Cấu trúc: Thành ngữ 'a change for the better' nghĩa là một sự cải thiện, thay đổi theo chiều hướng tốt lên. • Câu gốc: 'I think the weather will improve next week' (Tôi nghĩ thời tiết sẽ cải thiện vào tuần tới). • Chuyển đổi: 'I think there'll be a change for the better in the weather...' (Tôi nghĩ sẽ có sự thay đổi theo hướng tốt lên của thời tiết). • Ta điền cụm 'a change for the' kết hợp với từ 'better' đã có sẵn ở câu sau để hoàn chỉnh cấu trúc. questions.instruction:
Q45. Phoebe was surprised to be offered a place on the course.
CAME
The offer of a place on the course ______ Phoebe.
questions.explanation: Đáp án: CAME AS A SURPRISE TO. 📐 Cấu trúc: 'Something comes as a surprise to someone' nghĩa là cái gì xảy ra như một sự ngạc nhiên đối với ai, khiến ai ngạc nhiên. • Câu gốc: 'Phoebe was surprised to be offered...' (Phoebe đã ngạc nhiên khi được mời nhận một suất học...). • Chuyển đổi sang cấu trúc chủ ngữ chỉ vật: 'The offer of a place on the course came as a surprise to Phoebe.' (Lời mời nhập học đến như một sự ngạc nhiên đối với Phoebe). Source: CAE 3, Test 2, Page 50 Method: complete the gap with "CAME AS A SURPRISE TO" only after checking the words before and after it. Q46. If he doesn’t get that job, who knows what he'll do!

Cấu trúc này tương đương hoàn toàn với cụm từ nghi vấn 'who knows...' (ai mà biết được...). questions.instruction:

questions.instruction:
Q50. It is important to be well prepared for an interview because if you make a mistake, you may not get the job.
